subject: Science
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Science 8 Ecosystems Garden Add-On
grade_subject_fit: Science 8 ecosystem add-on using the school planter as a concrete anchor for observation, classification, evidence, variables, human impacts, biodiversity, and short conclusions.
unit_context: Students interact with the class planter and seed pucks daily. They observe plant growth, soil moisture, insects, sunlight, watering, seedling success, and human care choices in a Kamloops school garden context.
assignment_purpose: Expand the existing Science 8 human impacts on ecosystems fixture into a garden-based multi-day package with short, scaffolded science tasks.
final_evidence_target: Students make a short evidence-based recommendation for improving the planter ecosystem using observations, one data point, and one human-impact judgment.
student_task_flow: Observe the planter or use a provided garden case card.
Record a small amount of evidence.
Classify or explain one ecosystem relationship.
Use the evidence to make a short conclusion or recommendation.
success_criteria: I can make a careful garden observation.
I can use evidence instead of only opinion.
I can explain one way a human action affects the planter ecosystem.

supports_scaffolds.label: Concrete anchor
supports_scaffolds.support: Every task starts from the planter, seed pucks, data table, or a garden case card.

supports_scaffolds.label: Reduced writing
supports_scaffolds.support: Student responses are labels, short notes, or 2–4 sentence explanations.

supports_scaffolds.label: Aligned differentiation
supports_scaffolds.support: Level A records and labels; Level B explains with evidence; Level C compares or predicts.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use word banks, checkboxes, labelled diagrams, and one-sentence frames.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete the observation, evidence, and short explanation task.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare two garden choices or predict a likely ecosystem effect.

checkpoint_release_logic: Teacher checkpoints identify whether students are observing, classifying, and using evidence before the final recommendation.
teacher_implementation_notes: Use the real planter whenever possible. If the class cannot access it, use the provided sample observations.
Keep the focus on visible ecosystem evidence: plants, soil, water, light, insects, and human care choices.
Science 8 students can justify claims, but the evidence must be present and the writing structure visible.
Do not turn this into a full lab report. Keep conclusions short.
likely_misconceptions: Students may describe a plant without connecting it to ecosystem conditions.
Students may call every human impact harmful without considering mixed effects.
Students may confuse observation with inference.
Students may treat seed germination as guaranteed rather than an outcome with uncertainty.
pacing_shape: Six modules. Each module can run as one weekly science block with 10–30 minutes of daily garden observation.
assessment_focus: Observation, classification, evidence use, simple variables, ecosystem relationships, biodiversity, human impacts, and short evidence-based conclusions.

lesson_overview.title: Plan, Build, Grow — Science 8 Ecosystems Garden Add-On
lesson_overview.overview: Students use the planter as a small ecosystem model. They observe living and non-living parts, track changes, classify helpful and harmful human actions, identify variables, and use evidence to recommend one improvement to the garden.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How do living things, non-living factors, and human choices affect our planter ecosystem?
lesson_overview.big_idea: A garden is a small ecosystem where organisms, conditions, and human actions interact.

sequence.focus: Ecosystem parts: living, once-living, and non-living components.
Plant needs, limiting factors, and visible evidence.
Observation vs inference and simple variables.
Food webs, decomposers, insects, and biodiversity in the garden.
Human impacts: watering, weeding, pesticides, shade, soil, and habitat choices.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Students can work from direct observation, sample observation cards, or partner-supported recording while still answering the same science question.
integrity_checks: Student work stays grounded in observable garden evidence.
No full formal lab report is required.

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide — Science 8 Garden Ecosystems Add-On
teacher_guide.big_idea: The planter can function as a small ecosystem model for Science 8 when students observe concrete changes and connect them to living things, conditions, and human actions.
learning_goals: Identify living and non-living parts of a small ecosystem.
Use observations as evidence for a short explanation.
Describe one helpful, harmful, or mixed human impact on the planter ecosystem.
Make a short evidence-based recommendation for improving garden conditions.
materials: Ruler, watering container, and soil moisture notes
Garden case cards for indoor backup

timing.activity: Observe, water, count, measure, or record one garden condition.
Introduce the science idea with slides and one modelled example.
Complete the task sheet or organizer using planter evidence.
Exit check, partner share, or teacher checkpoint.

teacher_notes: Keep vocabulary concrete: organism, habitat, resource, evidence, variable, decomposer, biodiversity.
When students say something is good or bad, ask: what changed in the ecosystem?
Use sentence frames, but require at least one evidence word or number.
Do not overbuild the lab format. The goal is Science 8 reasoning, not formal report writing.
integrity_checks: Prompt students to name evidence before making a claim.
Keep written responses short enough to finish in class.
Use mixed-impact examples so students do not default to one-sided answers.

days.day_label: Module 1: What Is in Our Garden Ecosystem?

content.subtitle: Identify living and non-living parts of the planter.

rows: Living parts include plants, insects, and microbes.
Non-living parts include water, soil minerals, air, sunlight, and space.
Once-living parts, like dead leaves, can become food for decomposers.
Look closely at the planter.
Record three things you see.
Sort each item as living, once-living, or non-living.
Use one sentence to explain one choice.

task_sheet.title: Module 1 Task — Sort the Planter Ecosystem
instructions: Observe the planter or use the case card.
Record short notes, not full paragraphs.
Use the word bank if needed.

tasks.label: Part 1: Observe
tasks.prompt: Write or draw three things you notice in or near the planter.

tasks.label: Part 2: Sort
tasks.prompt: Sort your three items: living, once-living, or non-living. Explain one choice in one sentence.

tasks.label: Part 3: Ecosystem connection
tasks.prompt: Choose one item. How could it affect a plant in the planter?

embedded_supports: Word bank: plant, soil, water, sunlight, insect, dead leaf, air
Sentence frame: I sorted ___ as ___ because ___.
success_criteria: I explained one choice with evidence.

exit_ticket.title: Module 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Name one living and one non-living part of our planter ecosystem. Explain why both matter.

days.day_label: Module 2: Plant Needs and Limiting Factors

slides.title: Module 2: What Do Our Plants Need?

content.subtitle: Use evidence to decide which condition may be helping or limiting plant growth.

rows: A plant needs light, water, air, space, and nutrients.
A limiting factor is something there is not enough of.
Evidence helps us decide which factor matters most today.
Observe soil moisture, light, plant colour, and crowding.
Record one piece of evidence.
Choose one possible limiting factor.
Suggest one small action.

task_sheet.title: Module 2 Task — Plant Needs Evidence Check
instructions: Use your senses safely: look and touch soil only if your teacher says it is okay.
Record evidence before making a claim.

tasks.label: Part 1: Evidence table
tasks.prompt: Record one observation for each: soil moisture, sunlight, plant colour, space/crowding.

tasks.label: Part 2: Limiting factor
tasks.prompt: Which factor might be limiting growth today? Choose one and explain using your observation.

tasks.label: Part 3: Action
tasks.prompt: Suggest one small action: water, move, thin, shade, add support, or keep observing. Explain why.

embedded_supports: Sentence frame: The evidence I noticed was ___. This suggests ___ might be limiting growth.
Level A: choose from light, water, space, nutrients.
success_criteria: I recorded observations before making a claim.
I identified one possible limiting factor.

graphic_organizer.title: Plant Needs Evidence Organizer
rows: Water
Light
Space
Soil/nutrients
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use this table to connect conditions to plant growth.
success_criteria: I connected evidence to plant needs.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Plant Needs
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether students can separate observation from claim when identifying a limiting factor.
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move on when students can support one plant-needs claim with evidence.

content.subtitle: Separate what we observe from what we infer.

rows: An observation is what you directly notice or measure.
An inference is a conclusion based on observations.
A variable is something that can change in an investigation.
Record one observation.
Write one inference it could support.
Name one variable that might affect the result.
Keep the conclusion short.

task_sheet.title: Module 3 Task — Observation, Inference, Variable
instructions: Write exactly what you see first.
Then write what you think it may mean.
Name one variable that could change.

tasks.label: Part 1: Observation
tasks.prompt: Write one direct observation from the planter. Example: Two seedlings are shorter than the others.

tasks.label: Part 2: Inference
tasks.prompt: What might your observation mean? Use the frame: This might mean ___ because ___.

tasks.label: Part 3: Variable
tasks.prompt: Choose one variable that could affect plant growth: water, light, soil, space, temperature, or seed type. Explain in 1–2 sentences.

embedded_supports: Inference = what you think it means
success_criteria: I wrote an inference based on evidence.

exit_ticket.title: Module 3 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Write one observation and one inference about the planter. Label each one clearly.
success_criteria: My inference is connected to evidence.

days.day_label: Module 4: Food Webs, Decomposers, and Biodiversity

content.subtitle: Look for producers, consumers, decomposers, and signs of biodiversity.

rows: Producers make their own food using sunlight.
Consumers get energy by eating other organisms or plant material.
Decomposers break down once-living material and return nutrients to soil.
Choose a producer in the planter.
Add one possible consumer.
Add one decomposer or once-living material.
Explain why biodiversity can help an ecosystem.

task_sheet.title: Module 4 Task — Garden Food Web and Biodiversity
instructions: Use the word bank if you do not see every organism today.
Draw arrows to show energy movement.

tasks.label: Part 1: Roles
tasks.prompt: List one producer, one possible consumer, and one decomposer or decomposer food source in the garden.

tasks.label: Part 2: Food web
tasks.prompt: Draw a simple food chain or food web using at least three parts. Label each part.

tasks.label: Part 3: Biodiversity
tasks.prompt: Why might a planter with more plant types or insect visitors be more stable? Explain in 2–3 sentences.

embedded_supports: Sentence frame: More biodiversity can help because ___.
success_criteria: I made a simple food web connection.
I explained biodiversity using the garden.

graphic_organizer.title: Garden Ecosystem Roles Map
rows: Example 1
Example 2
Class observation
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use this organizer to connect organisms and evidence.
success_criteria: I included evidence or a reasonable example.

days.day_label: Module 5: Human Impacts on the Garden Ecosystem

slides.title: Module 5: Our Choices Change the Garden

content.subtitle: Judge human actions as mostly helpful, mostly harmful, or mixed using ecosystem evidence.

rows: Watering can help plants, but overwatering can harm roots.
Weeding can reduce competition, but removing habitat can affect insects.
Pesticides may protect plants, but can affect other organisms.
Choose one human action in the garden.
Decide whether it is mostly helpful, mostly harmful, or mixed.
Use one piece of evidence.
Suggest a better care choice if needed.

task_sheet.title: Module 5 Task — Human Impact Sort
instructions: Do not decide from opinion only.
Name what changes in the ecosystem.
Use mostly helpful, mostly harmful, or mixed.

tasks.label: Part 1: Sort actions
tasks.prompt: Sort these actions: watering daily, overwatering, adding mulch, using pesticide, planting native flowers, pulling all insects off plants.

tasks.label: Part 2: Evidence
tasks.prompt: Choose two actions. For each one, explain what ecosystem evidence would support your judgment.

tasks.label: Part 3: Better choice
tasks.prompt: Choose one mixed or harmful action. Suggest a better care choice for the planter.

embedded_supports: Judgment words: mostly helpful, mostly harmful, mixed
success_criteria: I explained what changes in the ecosystem.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Human Impacts
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether students use ecosystem evidence rather than personal preference.
look_fors: Allows mixed impacts when evidence supports it
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Ready for final recommendation when students can connect one human action to one ecosystem effect.

slides.title: Module 6: Recommend the Next Garden Step

content.subtitle: Use observations and evidence to recommend one action for the planter ecosystem.

rows: Name one action.
Use evidence from the planter or class data.
Explain how the action could affect the ecosystem.
Choose one recommendation.
Use one observation.
Use one data point or class result.
Explain if the impact is helpful, harmful, or mixed.

task_sheet.title: Module 6 Task — Prepare Your Recommendation
instructions: Use evidence from the garden or class data.
Keep your explanation short and clear.

tasks.label: Part 1: Choose
tasks.prompt: I recommend we should: water differently, add mulch, thin seedlings, move the planter, add native flowers, keep observing, or another teacher-approved action.

tasks.label: Part 2: Evidence
tasks.prompt: Write one observation and one data point or class result that supports your recommendation.

tasks.label: Part 3: Impact
tasks.prompt: Will your recommendation be mostly helpful, mostly harmful, or mixed? Explain using ecosystem evidence.

embedded_supports: Frame: I recommend ___. My evidence is ___. This could help because ___.
Use exact garden evidence when possible.
success_criteria: I used observation or data evidence.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Science 8 Garden Recommendation
final_response_sheet.prompt: Write a short recommendation for improving or maintaining the planter ecosystem. Use one observation, one data point or class result, and one explanation of the likely ecosystem impact.
planning_reminders: Explain the effect on plants, soil, water, insects, or habitat.

frame_strip: One observation that supports this is ___.
One data point or class result is ___.
This action would affect the ecosystem by ___.
paragraph_support.reminder_box: A science recommendation uses evidence, not only preference.

success_criteria: I used at least two pieces of evidence.

exit_ticket.title: Module 6 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: What is one thing you understand better now about how humans affect small ecosystems?
success_criteria: I connected it to the garden.
